mechanism_narrative: Persistent low inflation, as indicated by the Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL), signals a stable economic environment with predictable pricing power, reducing uncertainty for businesses and investors. This stability supports higher equity valuations by lowering perceived risk premiums, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: A statistical analysis reveals that S&P 500 20-day returns are not significantly positively correlated with periods of low CPIAUCSL readings (e.g., below 2.5% year-over-year growth) over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A significant and sustained increase in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) above a critical threshold indicates a deteriorating labor market, signaling reduced consumer confidence and spending. This contraction in demand negatively impacts corporate revenues and profits, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-day horizon.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day returns are positive in more than 40% of instances where the UNRATE rises by more than 0.5 percentage points over a 3-month period, or the average return is not statistically different from zero.

mechanism_narrative: Robust growth in retail sales (RSAFS) combined with a stable or declining federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) signifies strong consumer demand supported by accommodative monetary conditions. This environment fosters corporate revenue growth and lower borrowing costs, driving positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: A regression model fails to show a statistically significant positive coefficient for the interaction term between RSAFS growth and FEDFUNDS change when predicting S&P 500 20-day returns.

mechanism_narrative: During periods characterized by high inflation, strong GDP growth (GDPC1) can signal an overheating economy, which typically prompts the central bank to implement tighter monetary policy. This tightening increases discount rates and raises recession fears, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-day horizon.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day returns are not significantly negative when GDPC1 growth exceeds 3% annualized during periods where CPIAUCSL year-over-year growth is above 3%, as measured by a t-test (p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Consistent and positive month-over-month growth in non-farm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects a healthy and expanding labor market. This sustained job creation supports consumer income and spending, which directly contributes to corporate earnings and, consequently, positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 5-day horizon.
failure_condition: A statistical analysis shows that periods of sustained PAYEMS growth do not exhibit a statistically significant positive correlation with S&P 500 5-day returns over any 2-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) rises above a specific restrictive threshold, it indicates a tight monetary policy stance. This increases borrowing costs for businesses and consumers, dampening economic activity, reducing corporate profitability, and leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-day horizon.
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day returns are positive in more than 35% of instances where FEDFUNDS is above 4.0%, or the average return in such periods is not statistically different from zero.

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous and significant decline in industrial production (INDPRO) and housing starts (HOUST) signals a broad-based contraction across both manufacturing and construction sectors. This indicates a weakening economy with reduced corporate investment and consumer demand, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over 20 trading days.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO falls > 1% in 3 months AND HOUST falls > 10% in 3 months
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day returns are positive or flat in more than 45% of periods where both INDPRO falls by more than 1% and HOUST falls by more than 10% over a 3-month period.

mechanism_narrative: In an environment of low unemployment, a strong month-over-month increase in retail sales (RSAFS) signifies robust consumer confidence and discretionary spending power. This translates directly into higher corporate revenues and profits, driving positive S&P 500 forward returns over a 5-day horizon.
failure_condition: S&P 500 5-day returns are not significantly positive (t-test p > 0.05) when RSAFS growth exceeds 0.5% month-over-month during periods where the unemployment rate (UNRATE) is below 5%.
